title: PCI-DSS 10.4 — Time synchronisation: NTP drift on CDE hosts
exclusions: Does NOT replace NTP; surfaces CDE hosts whose measured offset from the authoritative time source exceeds 1s.
evidence: Saved search 'pci_10_4_ntp_drift' (every 15m) emits per host: hostname, offset_ms, stratum, last_ntp_sync, to compliance_summary.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'PCI-DSS 10.4 — Time synchronisation: NTP drift on CDE hosts' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper PCI-DSS control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the PCI-DSS control objective.

dataSources: `index=windows` sourcetype=w32time; `index=linux` sourcetype=chronyd OR sourcetype=ntpd; CDE lookup `cde_hosts.csv`.
app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833) (Splunkbase)
spl:
search (index=windows sourcetype=w32time) OR (index=linux sourcetype IN (chronyd,ntpd)) earliest=-15m
| stats latest(offset_ms) AS offset_ms latest(stratum) AS stratum latest(_time) AS last_ntp_sync BY hostname
| lookup cde_hosts.csv hostname OUTPUT in_cde
| where in_cde="yes" AND (abs(offset_ms)>1000 OR last_ntp_sync<now()-3600)
| table hostname offset_ms stratum last_ntp_sync
description: PCI-DSS 10.4 requires time synchronisation across CDE systems. This UC flags drift > 1s or NTP sync gaps > 1h.
value: Prevents the classic forensic nightmare of 'when exactly did the event occur' because the CDE clocks disagreed.
implementation: (1) Enforce w32time/chrony logs to Splunk; (2) maintain cde_hosts.csv; (3) schedule UC every 15m; (4) drift opens a Platform ticket; (5) quarterly sampling for auditor. Indexes required: `index=linux`, `index=windows`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=chronyd`, `sourcetype=ntpd`, `sourcetype=ntpd;`, `sourcetype=w32time`.
visualization: Scatter of offset_ms per host, table of drifted hosts, single value 'hosts over 1s drift'.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Authentication (CIM data model).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (Splunkbase app ID 833) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `windows`, `linux` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `cde_hosts.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('cde_hosts.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `10.4`. The control objective is: "Evidence that PCI-DSS 10.4 (Time synchronised) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.100: PCI-DSS 10.4 — Time synchronisation: NTP drift on CDE hosts." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (Splunkbase app ID 833), landing events in `windows`, `linux` under `w32time`. Scripted inputs at hourly cadence produce ~50-200 events/host/hour (~100-400 MB/host/month). Auditd log volume adds 500 MB - 2 GB/host/month.

Configure the input through Deployment Server > Forwarder Management > Splunk_TA_nix serverclass. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[script://./bin/openPorts.sh]
disabled = 0
interval = 3600
sourcetype = linux:network_listeners
index = linux
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `host, proto, port, process, pid, user, cmdline`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=windows earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Enforce w32time/chrony logs to Splunk. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** maintain cde_hosts.csv. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** schedule UC every 15m. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** drift opens a Platform ticket. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** quarterly sampling for auditor. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "PCI-DSS 10.4 — Time synchronisation: NTP drift on CDE hosts," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the PCI-DSS control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `cde_hosts.csv` — the authoritative Cardholder Data Environment (CDE) scope register. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`in_cde`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `hostname`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `cde_hosts.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval hostname = lower(hostname)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 15 minutes.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.11.100: PCI-DSS 10.4 — Time synchronisation: NTP drift on CDE hosts]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -15m
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.11.100,reg=PCI-DSS
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=windows sourcetype=w32time earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `hostname`, `offset_ms`, `stratum`, `_time`. Run:

```spl
index=windows sourcetype=w32time earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table hostname, offset_ms, stratum, _time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `cde_hosts.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup cde_hosts.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.11.100: PCI-DSS evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Scatter Of Offset Ms Per Host (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of Drifted Hosts (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value 'Hosts Over 1S Drift' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**PCI-DSS-specific failure modes:**

• Scope creep: PCI Requirement 1.2 requires CDE (Cardholder Data Environment) documentation. Every PCI DSS detection must explicitly reference the CDE inventory lookup; an 'all hosts' search that happens to catch CDE hosts is NOT compliant evidence — the QSA needs to see the scope boundary enforced in the query itself.

• Log retention vs online availability: Req 10.5.1 requires 1 year of audit log retention with at least 3 months immediately available. The index's `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` must be >= 31536000; additionally, Splunk's summary index or an accelerated datamodel must cover the last 90 days for immediate auditor retrieval. One-year hot/warm is not required; the distinction matters for cost.

• Daily log review (Req 10.4): PCI requires DAILY review evidence. Detections backing this control must produce signed evidence per calendar day, not per 24-hour rolling window — a search scheduled at 23:55 daily is compliant; one running every 24h from an arbitrary start time is not.

• FIM (File Integrity Monitoring, Req 11.5.2) scope: FIM detections must cover critical system files on CDE devices, not 'all changes anywhere.' Use the CDE host list as the first-pass filter; flags on non-CDE hosts dilute the signal and are out of scope for the RoC.
grandmaExplanation: We pCI-DSS 10.4 requires time synchronisation across CDE systems. PCI DSS is about protecting card and payment data when we run these checks.
